note_data.title: Incomplete Records
keyPoints: **Incomplete records** are records kept by businesses that do not follow the full double-entry bookkeeping system
**Single entry system** records only one aspect of each transaction, usually cash receipts and payments
**Opening capital** is calculated by preparing a statement of financial position at the start of the period
**Closing capital** is calculated by preparing a statement of financial position at the end of the period
**Net profit** is calculated using the formula: Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings - Additional Capital
**Margin** is profit expressed as a percentage of selling price: (Profit ÷ Selling Price) × 100
**Mark-up** is profit expressed as a percentage of cost price: (Profit ÷ Cost Price) × 100
Missing figures are calculated by preparing control accounts for debtors, creditors, and cash
Total sales and purchases can be calculated using debtors and creditors control accounts when cash sales/purchases are given

definitions.definition: A system of recording transactions that does not follow the complete double-entry principle, usually recording only cash receipts and payments
A bookkeeping system that records only one aspect of each transaction, typically the cash effect
Profit expressed as a percentage of selling price
Profit expressed as a percentage of cost price
Total assets minus total liabilities, which equals capital

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings clearly. Use the net assets method for capital or control accounts for missing figures.
Set out the statement/account with proper headings and show all calculations step by step
Give the reason why incomplete records are used, such as 'because the business owner lacks bookkeeping knowledge'

modelAnswer.question: Ali's business uses incomplete records. His assets and liabilities were: 1 Jan 2023: Cash $2,000, Inventory $5,000, Debtors $3,000, Creditors $1,500. 31 Dec 2023: Cash $3,500, Inventory $6,000, Debtors $4,000, Creditors $2,000. During the year he withdrew $12,000 and introduced additional capital of $5,000. Calculate his net profit for 2023.
modelAnswer.answer:
Opening Capital = Assets - Liabilities = (2,000 + 5,000 + 3,000) - 1,500 = $8,500
Closing Capital = Assets - Liabilities = (3,500 + 6,000 + 4,000) - 2,000 = $11,500
Net Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings - Additional Capital
Net Profit = 11,500 - 8,500 + 12,000 - 5,000 = $10,000

commonMistakes: Never confuse margin with mark-up — margin uses selling price as denominator, mark-up uses cost price
Students often forget to add drawings when calculating profit using the capital comparison method
Never subtract additional capital introduced during the year — it should be subtracted from the profit calculation
Students frequently calculate opening/closing capital incorrectly by adding liabilities instead of subtracting them
Never use credit sales figure when asked for total sales — must include cash sales using debtors control account
examinerTips: This topic appears in Paper 2 almost every year, usually worth 15-20 marks
Always prepare a statement of financial position first to find opening and closing capital
Show all workings clearly for margin and mark-up calculations — partial marks are available
When preparing control accounts, ensure the missing figure balances the account
formulas: Net Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings - Additional Capital
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Statement of Financial Position layout showing Assets (Non-current assets + Current assets) minus Liabilities (Current liabilities + Non-current liabilities) equals Capital, with clear labeling for opening and closing positions
